Client 2: Same. And just… plain English would be great. I get stressed when it gets too technical.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. David, can you give me the big-picture household income—what you’d call your gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 222550 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s combining your self-employed income streams plus Patrici’s W-2 type income and anything else coming in.
Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right. Mine’s steady, his is the one that jumps around.

Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right. Mine’s steady, his is the one that jumps around.
Advisor: Okay. Next, on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, just to keep life going? Mortgage, utilities, food, everything.
Client 2: We’re around seventeen thousand a month—sorry, I mean the exact number we’ve been using is 16950 per month.
Advisor: Perfect, I’ll write down 16950 monthly for total expenses. Now within that, how much is debt payments each month—so mortgage plus any other loans?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one, and then there’s that other loan. Together it’s 6700 a month.

Client 2: We’re around seventeen thousand a month—sorry, I mean the exact number we’ve been using is 16950 per month.
Advisor: Perfect, I’ll write down 16950 monthly for total expenses. Now within that, how much is debt payments each month—so mortgage plus any other loans?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one, and then there’s that other loan. Together it’s 6700 a month.
Client 1: Yeah—$6,200 on the mortgage and $500 on the other loan, so 6700 total monthly debt payments.
Advisor: Great, thank you. We’ll come back and list each one in a second. Before that, I want to understand your investable assets—so not the house, but accounts you can invest from. What’s the total you’re tracking?

Client 1: Yeah—$6,200 on the mortgage and $500 on the other loan, so 6700 total monthly debt payments.
Advisor: Great, thank you. We’ll come back and list each one in a second. Before that, I want to understand your investable assets—so not the house, but accounts you can invest from. What’s the total you’re tracking?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 936600.
Advisor: Okay—936600. Let’s break that down so I’m not missing anything. What are the main buckets you think of?
Client 1: We’ve got a joint taxable brokerage at 395150.

Client 2: I have a private markets account—mine—141700.
Advisor: Great. And then separate from investable assets, you mentioned the house—what are you using as the current value for your primary home?
Client 1: We have it at 189300.
Advisor: Thanks. Now let’s list the debts so the 6700 monthly makes sense. First, the mortgage—what’s the current balance, the rate, and the monthly payment?
Client 1: Mortgage balance is 142950, the interest rate is 3.17%, and the monthly payment is 6200.

Client 2: Yep.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. I’m going to zoom in on the debts for a second. Between the mortgage and that other loan, what do you consider your total loans outstanding right now—just the balances added together?
Client 1: Total loans outstanding is 145750.
Advisor: Got it. And just to sanity-check the pieces: the mortgage balance itself is what number today?
Client 1: Mortgage balance outstanding is 142950.

Client 2: And there’s cash—well, like a bank-type account we use as a buffer—91,000.
Client 1: Right, 91000 in that joint account. Then I’ve got my retirement account—401k/IRA style—at 308800.
Client 2: I have a private markets account—mine—141700.
Advisor: Great. And then separate from investable assets, you mentioned the house—what are you using as the current value for your primary home?
Client 1: We have it at 189300.

Client 1: Yes—2037-02-07.
Advisor: Great. And the other loan—what is that one, and what are the key numbers: balance, rate, payment, and when it’s scheduled to be done?
Client 2: It’s just a loan—balance is 2850, rate is 13.61%, payment is 500 a month, and the final payment date is 2032-01-03.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. That helps explain why you feel the fixed obligations pretty sharply month to month.
Advisor: Switching gears to protection—David, do you have life insurance in place right now?

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. For me, it’s getting the numbers organized—income, investments, debts—and then clear next steps. I don’t want a sales pitch.
Client 2: Same. And just… plain English would be great. I get stressed when it gets too technical.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. David, can you give me the big-picture household income—what you’d call your gross income for the year, all sources combined?

Client 2: We have 1 dependent.
Advisor: Okay. And just to place the tax picture in the right ballpark—what bracket are you typically landing in these days?
Client 1: We’re in the 35% bracket.
Advisor: Thanks. And country-wise—just confirming for planning and accounts—are you based in the US?
Client 2: Yes, US.

Advisor: Alright. So we’ve got income covered, assets covered, debts covered, and your life insurance. Next time we’ll dig into goals and timelines, but for today I think we’ve got the core numbers down.
Client 2: That sounds good—thank you.
Advisor: Of course. Before we wrap, I just want to fill in a couple household basics so I’m not making assumptions. David, Patrici—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you—how many dependents do you have?

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. For me, it’s getting the numbers organized—income, investments, debts—and then clear next steps. I don’t want a sales pitch.
Client 2: Same. And just… plain English would be great. I get stressed when it gets too technical.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. David, can you give me the big-picture household income—what you’d call your gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 222550 a year.

Advisor: Totally. Next meeting we’ll map goals and timelines, but today we’ve got the core picture: income streams, the investable accounts, the home and mortgage, the other loan, and your life policy.
Client 2: Sounds good. I appreciate keeping it straightforward.
Advisor: Before we hit stop, just a couple of household basics I still need so I don’t assume wrong things. First—risk comfort. When markets drop, would you say you’re conservative, moderate, or aggressive overall?
Client 1: We’re moderate.
Client 2: Yeah, I’d say moderate—like, we can handle some ups and downs, but not anything wild.

Advisor: Got it. And household-wise, is it just the two of you as adults in the home—so two adults total?
Client 2: Yes, 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is just about how we’d work together. Are you engaging me through an RIA—registered investment advisor relationship—or is this through a bank or insurance company channel?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. And last admin-type item: what state do you consider your primary residence for—where you’re legally resident?

Advisor: Okay. Next, on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, just to keep life going? Mortgage, utilities, food, everything.
Client 2: We’re around seventeen thousand a month—sorry, I mean the exact number we’ve been using is 16950 per month.

Client 2: The mortgage is the big one, and then there’s that other loan. Together it’s 6700 a month.
Client 1: Yeah—$6,200 on the mortgage and $500 on the other loan, so 6700 total monthly debt payments.

Client 2: Correct, I’m employed.
Advisor: Okay. And what’s your gross annual income from that job—ballpark is fine, but I’d love the exact if you know it.
Client 2: It’s basically around sixty, but the exact is 59400.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And just so I label things correctly in our notes when we’re looking at accounts and income lines: David is Client 1 and you’re Client 2, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m Client 2.

Advisor: Actually—one last thing before we fully wrap, Patrici. I want to make sure I’ve got your details right, not just the household totals.
Client 2: Sure, yeah.
Advisor: In the household, your role is spouse or partner to David, right?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And employment-wise, you’re currently employed—W-2 job, not self-employed like David?
